programs.name: DenaliCare
programs.what_it_does: DenaliCare is Alaska's Medicaid program providing health coverage for low-income seniors and disabled individuals, including some coverage for limited expenses in senior living facilities. It helps pay for medical services to support long-term care needs.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors aged 65+ and disabled adults who meet Alaska's Medicaid income and asset limits, U.S. citizens or qualified aliens residing in Alaska.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by services covered; includes doctor visits, hospital care, prescriptions, and limited long-term care support. Apply online or at Public Assistance Offices for full details.[3]

programs.name: Adults with Physical & Developmental Disabilities Waiver (APDD)
programs.what_it_does: The APDD waiver provides home and community-based services to help eligible adults avoid nursing home placement. Services include home modifications and personal care assistance.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Adults over 21 with physical or developmental disabilities requiring nursing home level of care, including eligible seniors.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Personal care, home modifications, respite care, and other supports tailored to individual needs; value varies by assessment.[3]

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.what_it_does: PACE provides comprehensive medical and social services to frail seniors in community settings, including adult day health care and home care. It coordinates all Medicare and Medicaid-covered services plus additional support.
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 55+ who are nursing home eligible, live in a PACE service area, and can safely be managed in the community.
programs.benefit_value: Full capitation coverage for all needed services; no additional premiums for full Medicaid dual eligibles.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI, QDWI)
programs.what_it_does: These programs help pay Medicare Part A/B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copays for eligible low-income seniors. Alaska has higher income limits than the contiguous 48 states.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Medicare enrollees aged 65+ with low income and limited resources; specific limits vary by program (e.g., QMB lowest income, QI highest).[3]
programs.benefit_value: QMB: Full Part A/B costs; SLMB/QI: Part B premiums ($174.70/mo in 2024); QDWI: Part A premiums for working disabled.[3]

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.what_it_does: SNAP provides monthly benefits via EBT card to buy food at approved stores. It helps low-income households, including seniors, afford nutritious meals.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ or households with elderly/disabled members meeting income/resource tests; Alaska residents.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per household; maximum varies by size/income (e.g., $1,751/mo for household of 4 in 2024 FY).[4]

programs.name: Heating Assistance Program (HAP)
programs.what_it_does: HAP provides one-time cash payments to help eligible households pay heating costs during winter. It targets low-income Alaskans facing high energy expenses.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors 65+ meeting income guidelines (up to 150-200% FPG); Alaska residents.[4]
programs.benefit_value: $300-$1,000 one-time payment depending on region, fuel type, and income (e.g., higher in rural Alaska).[4]

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: The program funds home energy efficiency improvements like insulation and sealing to reduce heating costs. It prioritizes low-income and vulnerable households including seniors.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income homeowners/renters, elderly, disabled; income at or below 200% FPG.
programs.benefit_value: Free services worth $4,000-$7,000 per home; average annual energy savings of 20-30%.

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: SHIP offers free counseling on Medicare options, including Parts A/B/D, Medigap, and savings programs. Alaska's SHIP helps seniors navigate coverage and costs.
programs.who_its_for: All Medicare beneficiaries aged 65+, caregivers, and family members in Alaska.
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; average savings identified $600+/year per client.

programs.name: Alaska Meals on Wheels
programs.what_it_does: Provides home-delivered nutritious meals to homebound seniors to promote independence. Often coordinates with local senior centers and Area Agencies on Aging.
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals, typically low-income or with health needs.
programs.benefit_value: 5-14 meals/week at $5-8/suggested donation; full subsidy for qualifying low-income.

programs.name: Alzheimer's Family Caregiver Support Program
programs.what_it_does: Offers respite care, counseling, and training to caregivers of seniors with Alzheimer's or related dementias. Helps prevent caregiver burnout and support family caregiving.
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of Alaska seniors 60+ with Alzheimer's/dementia.
programs.benefit_value: Respite grants up to $1,500/year; free support groups and education.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides part-time community service jobs with training to unemployed low-income seniors. Aims to help transition to unsubsidized employment.
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income seniors 55+ (income ≤125% FPG); preference for veterans, disabled.
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage jobs (20 hrs/wk); work experience, training, supportive services.

programs.name: Alaska Legal Services Corporation (ALSC) Senior Legal Services
programs.what_it_does: Provides free civil legal aid to low-income seniors for issues like benefits, housing, guardianship. Operates statewide with priority for elders.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ (≤125% FPG) facing civil legal problems; Alaska residents.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Free legal representation, advice, self-help; serves thousands annually statewide.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities, investigating complaints and ensuring quality care. Provides confidential assistance to resolve issues.
programs.who_its_for: Residents of nursing homes, assisted living, and similar facilities in Alaska; families.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy services; resolves 80%+ of complaints without formal action.

programs.name: Alaska Senior Benefits Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides monthly cash assistance to Alaskans age 65 and older with low to moderate income. Payment amounts are $250, $175, or $76 per month depending on income level and marital status.
programs.who_its_for: Alaska residents age 65 or older who are U.S. citizens or qualified immigrants, have a social security number, and meet income limits tied to Alaska Federal Poverty Guidelines.
programs.whats_unique: This is a state-funded program established in 2007 that replaced the Alaska Longevity Bonus Program (1972-2003), which was unique to Alaska. The program is needs-based and considers only income, not savings, for eligibility determination.
programs.benefit_value: $76–$250 per month depending on income and marital status

programs.name: Adult Public Assistance (APA) Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides cash assistance to aged, blind, or disabled Alaskans to help them remain independent. The program gives cash to SSI recipients and others who meet income and resource limits.
programs.who_its_for: Alaskans age 65 or older (or age 18+ if blind or disabled), who are U.S. citizens or qualified immigrants and Alaska residents with resources not exceeding $2,000 for individuals or $3,000 for couples.
programs.whats_unique: This is a state-funded supplemental assistance program that works alongside federal SSI benefits to support low-income seniors and disabled individuals in Alaska.
programs.benefit_value: Varies based on individual needs and income limits
